v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Re-binds on every render if handler changes, no target option, no types

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Handler must be stable (useCallback), otherwise rebinds; no types per event

v3_senior.code:
// Stable handler via ref
function useEventListener<K extends keyof WindowEventMap>(event:K,handler:(e:WindowEventMap[K])=>void){const ref=useRef(handler);useEffect(()=>{ref.current=handler});useEffect(()=>{const wrapped=(e:WindowEventMap[K])=>ref.current(e);window.addEventListener(event,wrapped);return()=>window.removeEventListener(event,wrapped)},[event])}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Latest handler without rebinding (ref pattern), typed event map, single subscribe

v4_staff.code:
// React 19 useEffectEvent (when stable)
const onResize=useEffectEvent(()=>updateLayout(measurements));useEffect(()=>{window.addEventListener('resize',onResize);return()=>window.removeEventListener('resize',onResize)},[])
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: useEffectEvent eliminates the ref pattern, captures latest values without rebinding

v5_principal.code:
// Often the wrong primitive. Prefer: CSS hover/focus, ResizeObserver, IntersectionObserver, native form events. Global window listeners are last resort.
// Pair with AbortSignal for cleanup: addEventListener(...,{signal})
v5_principal.the_realization: useEventListener is convenient but encourages global listeners. Modern web platform has scoped observers (Resize, Intersection, Mutation) and CSS for most use cases. Reach for window listeners last, not first.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 if needed, v4 React 19, v5 reconsider

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Locale (comma vs period), no formatting, can enter negative, scroll wheel changes value, no currency symbol

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Cursor jumps on format, no live formatting, decimal places not enforced

v3_senior.code:
// Use input-mask library + Intl.NumberFormat
import {NumericFormat} from 'react-number-format';<NumericFormat thousandSeparator decimalScale={2} prefix='$' onValueChange={({floatValue})=>setAmount(floatValue)}/>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Real-time formatting, cursor preserved, locale, decimal enforcement

v4_staff.code:
// Money as type (not number). Avoid floating point — use minor units (cents) or Decimal.
type Money={amount:bigint;currency:string};const m:Money={amount:1099n,currency:'USD'} // $10.99
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Money never as float (rounding errors), BigInt for precision, currency tagged

v5_principal.code:
// Money is a value object. Operations validated (can't add USD+EUR without conversion). Display vs storage formatting separate. dinero.js / money.js for ops.
import Dinero from 'dinero.js';const total=Dinero({amount:1099,currency:'USD'}).add(Dinero({amount:200,currency:'USD'}))
v5_principal.the_realization: Money in JavaScript is a notorious source of bugs (0.1+0.2 !== 0.3). Modeling it as a domain object with operations, currency-tagged, integer-based eliminates a class of bugs. Input is the visible part; the type matters more.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum for UI, v4-v5 for finance apps

scenario: Show cached data instantly, refresh in background

v1_junior.code: // Fetch fresh on every visit, show spinner
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Slow perceived performance, repeated work, no offline

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No staleness indicator, no cache expiration, hydration mismatch

v3_senior.code:
// React Query with stale/gc times
useQuery({queryKey,queryFn,staleTime:1000*60,gcTime:1000*60*60})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Stale-while-revalidate built-in, cache reused across components, deduped fetches

v4_staff.code:
// Persistence + SSR hydration
import {hydrate,dehydrate} from '@tanstack/react-query';// Server prefetches, client hydrates with same cache
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: SSR + client cache aligned, no waterfall, no hydration mismatch

v5_principal.code:
// SWR is one strategy. Compare: cache-first (offline), network-first (always-fresh), stale-while-revalidate (balance). Choose per query based on freshness need.
// RSC turns this into server concern: server decides cache strategy.
v5_principal.the_realization: SWR feels magical but isn't always right. Stock prices need network-first; product lists are fine with SWR; user settings work with cache-first. The choice is product, not technical default.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for SSR, v5 for serious products

scenario: Open auth/OAuth in popup or iframe

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Lost reference, no communication back, popup blocker, no fallback

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: COOP/COEP blocks .closed check, no message passing, no result

v3_senior.code:
// postMessage for communication
window.addEventListener('message',e=>{if(e.origin!==expectedOrigin)return;if(e.data.type==='auth-complete'){popup.close();onSuccess(e.data.token)}})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Origin-checked message passing, secure, no polling

v4_staff.code:
// Window Management API for proper popup handling. Channel Messaging for typed comms.
const channel=new MessageChannel();popup.postMessage('init',[channel.port2]);channel.port1.onmessage=...
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Channel-based for type safety, modern APIs

v5_principal.code:
// OAuth = redirect flow, not popup. PKCE in same window. Avoid popups (blockers, mobile UX). Use silent renewal in iframe for token refresh only.
// For embedding: postMessage with origin allow-list + iframe with sandbox attrs.
v5_principal.the_realization: Popups have been hostile to web UX for 20 years. Redirect flows are universally better for auth. Popups remain only for very specific cases (silent token refresh). Engineers reach for window.open from habit.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 if must use popup, v5 prefer redirect

scenario: User drags column edge to resize

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No manual control, overflow content cut

v2_mid_level.code:
// Drag handle at column edge
onMouseDown:(col)=>{const startX=e.clientX;const startW=widths[col];onMove:(e)=>setWidths({...widths,[col]:startW+e.clientX-startX})}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No persist, no min/max, no double-click to fit, no touch

v3_senior.code:
// Persist + min/max + double-click auto-fit (measure content)
onDoubleClick:()=>{const widest=measureColumn(col);setWidths({...,[col]:widest})}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Persisted preferences, smart auto-fit, touch/keyboard alternatives

v4_staff.code:
// TanStack Table column resize
const tbl=useReactTable({columns,data,enableColumnResizing:true,columnResizeMode:'onChange'})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles edge cases, virtualization-compatible, accessibility built-in

v5_principal.code:
// Columns are user-customizable: order, width, visibility, pinning. Saved as views per user. Shareable view URLs. Defaults per role.
// Data tables = mini-spreadsheets, treat as such.
v5_principal.the_realization: Column customization is the wedge into 'tables as workspaces'. Once columns persist, you add view sharing, view presets, group-by. Engineers ship resize and stop; users want the whole spreadsheet.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 with TanStack, v5 for data-heavy SaaS

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Spammy, expensive on long lists, no debounce

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: 300ms always feels laggy, doesn't respect users who want explicit submit

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Cheap operations live, expensive on commit, both feel responsive

v4_staff.code:
// Inline suggestions while typing (cheap), full results on enter (commit). Tab to select suggestion.
// Pattern: Spotlight / Algolia InstantSearch
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Two-tier UX, suggestions guide query refinement, full results when committed

v5_principal.code: // Search modes are product decisions. Algolia/Meilisearch are fast enough for live; databases aren't. Match technology to UX. Sometimes the right answer: invest in faster search index, not in clever debouncing.
v5_principal.the_realization: Search-as-you-type works when the search is sub-100ms; otherwise it's frustrating. Database queries usually aren't fast enough; dedicated search engines (Algolia, Typesense, Meili) are. The infrastructure choice determines the UX.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for SaaS, v5 strategic

scenario: User takes photo directly in app

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Desktop falls back to file picker (fine), but no in-app capture, can't crop in-flow

v2_mid_level.code:
// getUserMedia for in-app camera
const stream=await navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({video:{facingMode:'environment'}});video.srcObject=stream
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Permissions UX poor, no torch, no zoom, no scan-line for documents

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Hardware features (torch, zoom), high-res capture, document detection

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Computer vision features (BarcodeDetector, FaceDetector — Shape Detection API), document UX

v5_principal.code:
// Native camera is product. Live filters (WebGL shaders), real-time AR (face filters), receipt scanning (OCR), AR shopping. The web platform has matured to compete with native.
// Worth investing if mobile-first product.
v5_principal.the_realization: Web camera capture has gone from 'take a photo' to 'AR-grade'. BarcodeDetector, MediaPipe, WebGL shaders, ImageCapture — engineers who haven't looked recently are surprised what's possible. Native-class camera UX is achievable on web.

when_to_use_which_version: v2 for in-app, v3+ for serious mobile-first

scenario: Show placeholder background matching image colors

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Jarring transition to image, no perceived speed

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Manual, doesn't scale, no automation

v3_senior.code:
// Server-side: extract on upload using sharp
const {dominant}=await sharp(buffer).stats();// Or use color-thief / vibrant.js client-side
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Automated extraction, stored as metadata, used as bg-color while loading

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Better than solid color — gradient/shape approximation, 30 bytes vs 5KB image

v5_principal.code:
// Image loading is perceptual psychology. LQIP > blurhash > thumbhash > solid color, in fidelity. Browser native (img loading='lazy' decoding='async' fetchpriority) handles physics; placeholders handle perception.
// Combine with View Transitions for smooth reveal.
v5_principal.the_realization: Loading is psychological, not just temporal. ThumbHash makes 5KB image feel like instant load via 30-byte placeholder. The investment in tooling (extract on upload, decode on client) pays back in perceived speed which converts.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for media-heavy

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Looks ugly, no info, just opacity ghost

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Off-screen element must be in DOM, sizing tricky, no interaction

v3_senior.code:
// dnd-kit DragOverlay — render outside DOM hierarchy, follows cursor naturally
<DndContext><DragOverlay>{active&&<CardPreview {...active}/>}</DragOverlay></DndContext>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Decouples preview from source, custom UX (badge showing count, snapping, scaling)

v4_staff.code:
// Multi-select drag preview: 'dragging 3 items'
<DragOverlay>{selected.length>1?<Stack count={selected.length}/>:<Card {...selected[0]}/>}</DragOverlay>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Multi-select awareness, contextual preview, drop validity indicators

v5_principal.code:
// Drag preview communicates intent. Card on drop zone valid? Show 'drop here' state on overlay. Invalid? Strike-through. Reorder vs reparent? Different cursors.
// Preview is part of the affordance system.
v5_principal.the_realization: Drag preview isn't decoration — it's communication. What's being dragged (single/multi), where it can drop (valid zones), what'll happen (move/copy). Default ghost communicates none of that.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for polished

scenario: List of recent activities (likes, comments, follows)

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No grouping ('Alice and 3 others liked'), no read state, no realtime

v2_mid_level.code:
// Group by actor + verb + object
function groupActivities(arr){return groupBy(arr,a=>`${a.verb}-${a.objectId}`)}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Group key arbitrary, time windows wrong (all-time vs hourly), no realtime

v3_senior.code:
// Time-bucketed grouping: same verb+object within 1hr group
// Plus: 'Alice liked your post' vs 'Alice and 3 others liked your post' format
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Time-aware grouping, natural language formatting, read state, realtime via WS

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Standard spec, federation-ready (ActivityPub), composable with Mastodon-like ecosystems

v5_principal.code:
// Feeds are product surface — ranking matters more than recency. ML ranking, diversity, relevance. Treat as ranked list, not stream.
// Cassandra/Redis for fan-out at scale.
v5_principal.the_realization: Activity feeds appear as 'list of events' but are ranking/recommendation problems at scale. Twitter/Instagram rank, group, filter. Naive chronological feeds get abandoned. Feed = product = ML problem.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for serious, v4-v5 for social products

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Email compromised? Locked out. No identity verification. Social-engineering target.

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Security questions widely guessable, no real verification

v3_senior.code: // Multiple paths: email, backup codes (downloaded at 2FA setup), passkey on second device, verified phone number — user picks
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Multiple verification paths, user-chosen, attack-surface aware

v4_staff.code: // Step-up recovery: identity verification (ID document upload + selfie) for high-stakes accounts. Waiting period for sensitive changes.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Risk-tiered recovery, IDV for high-value accounts, cool-down on sensitive ops

v5_principal.code:
// Recovery = attack vector. Design as adversarial: assume attacker has email, phone. What recovery survives? Hardware key + biometric. Recovery legal: GDPR identity verification, dispute process.
// Threat model > convenience.
v5_principal.the_realization: Recovery is the most-attacked auth surface. Every shortcut (security questions, SMS) is an attack path. Designing recovery is designing for adversary — convenience for users means attackers too. Hard problem; not where to cut corners.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for accounts with value, v5 always think adversarially

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for User prints page — looks reasonable: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Sidebars print, ads print, dark backgrounds eat ink

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Page breaks bad, links lose URL info, no page numbers

v3_senior.code:
// page-break-after on h2, anchor URLs printed via ::after
a::after{content:' (' attr(href) ')'}h2{page-break-before:always}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Print-aware breaks, anchor URLs included, no clipped content

v4_staff.code:
// CSS Paged Media: @page, page numbers, headers/footers
@page{margin:1in;@bottom-right{content:counter(page) ' of ' counter(pages)}}
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Real document quality, page-aware, professional output

v5_principal.code:
// Print is one output medium. PDF generation server-side (Puppeteer/Playwright) for reliable cross-browser. Templates with CSS. Same source, multiple outputs.
// Often forget print; in B2B (invoices, reports) it's critical.
v5_principal.the_realization: Print fidelity is the difference between consumer app and B2B-grade. Invoice that prints perfectly, report you can hand to a client. Engineers who say 'who prints?' miss enterprise UX.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for B2B, v5 for document-heavy

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Status code still 200 (SPA), no SEO impact, no helpful suggestions

v2_mid_level.code:
// SSR sets 404 status code
export async function loader(){throw new Response('Not Found',{status:404})}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Generic 404, no suggestion, no search, no recent items

v3_senior.code:
// Helpful 404: 'Did you mean?' (Levenshtein on similar routes), recent items, search box
const suggestions=findSimilar(badPath,validPaths,maxDistance:2)
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Recovery-focused UX, suggestions, search, navigation back to value

v4_staff.code:
// Differentiate: bad URL (404), no permission (403), gone (410), private (sign in). Each has different UX.
// Track 404s — they reveal broken external links, bot probing, redesign breakages
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Status precision, monitoring 404 patterns, redirect strategy for moved content

v5_principal.code:
// 404 is product feedback. Frequent 404 = users want something missing, or links broke. Track + redirect or build the missing page. Personalized 404 (recent activity).
// Plus: SEO impact — 404s in Search Console mean lost traffic.
v5_principal.the_realization: 404 is information. Bot probing → security signal. Old URLs → redirect candidates. Common typos → maybe build the page. Engineers treat 404 as fallback; product teams treat it as data.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious products

scenario: Promise rejects in event handler — how does user know?

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Rejection silently swallowed, user thinks it worked

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Repetitive, raw error messages, no retry, no Sentry

v3_senior.code:
// Mutation hook centralizes
function useSafeMutation(fn){return useMutation({mutationFn:fn,onSuccess:()=>toast.success('Saved'),onError:(e)=>{toast.error(messageFor(e));captureException(e)}})}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Centralized error handling, telemetry, friendly messages

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Errors as types, compiler enforces handling, no swallowed promises

v5_principal.code:
// Errors are part of product spec. Each error has: code, user-message, suggested-action, severity, recovery-flow. PMs design errors as carefully as success states.
// Effect-TS / Neverthrow for typed error channels.
v5_principal.the_realization: Async errors silently swallowed is the #1 source of 'works on my machine but users report broken'. Result types eliminate this category. Engineers default to throw + try-catch and lose information; typed errors preserve it.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4 for serious apps, v5 for SaaS

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Not reported, no source maps, no context

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Minified stack traces unreadable, no breadcrumbs, no user context

v3_senior.code:
// Sentry / similar
import * as Sentry from '@sentry/react';Sentry.init({dsn,tracesSampleRate:0.1});// Captures with source maps, breadcrumbs, user context
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Source maps de-obfuscate, breadcrumbs reproduce, user/session context, deduplication

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Rich context drives faster triage, A/B-aware bug grouping

v5_principal.code:
// Errors are signal in a feedback loop. Track: errors per user, error budget per release, regression detection on deploy, auto-rollback on error spike. Errors → SLO.
// Session replay (LogRocket/FullStory) shows reproduction.
v5_principal.the_realization: Error tracking is half: capture. Other half: action. SLO-based budgets, deploy alerts, auto-rollback, session replay. Without action layer, dashboards are vanity. With it, error tracking is engineering culture.

where_most_engineers_stop: v2 — POST to endpoint, no source maps
when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for serious products

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Drifts (setInterval inaccurate), continues past 0, no cleanup, hidden tab pauses

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Drift over time, hidden-tab throttling makes it slow, multi-tab inconsistent

v3_senior.code:
// Target time, recompute remaining
const target=Date.now()+60000;useEffect(()=>{const id=setInterval(()=>{const remaining=Math.max(0,target-Date.now());setS(Math.ceil(remaining/1000));if(remaining<=0)clearInterval(id)},1000);return()=>clearInterval(id)},[])
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Target-time based (no drift), hidden tabs recover correct value, multi-tab consistent

v4_staff.code:
// Server-authoritative for SLAs (auction ending, contest deadline). Client time can be wrong.
const target=serverNow+serverDelta
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Server time authoritative, client clock drift handled, fair for all users

v5_principal.code:
// Time is hard. NTP, clock skew, time zones, DST. Use Temporal API (when stable) for time math. For countdowns to real events: WS push the trigger, don't rely on client time.
// 'Auction ending' = preload or Early Hintses 'closed' event, not client checks time.
v5_principal.the_realization: Client-side countdowns are display only — never authoritative. The end of an auction is when the server says so, not when the client's timer hits zero. Engineers who rely on client time get exploited by clock manipulation.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for fairness-critical

scenario: International phone number with country code

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No format, no country, no validation

v2_mid_level.code:
// Two fields: country select + number
<select><option value='+1'>US</option></select><input type='tel'/>
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Country code drift, formatting per country missing, validation by country missing

v3_senior.code:
// libphonenumber-js
import {parsePhoneNumberFromString} from 'libphonenumber-js';const p=parsePhoneNumberFromString(input,'US');p.isValid();p.formatInternational()
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Real phone parsing/validation/formatting, country-aware

v4_staff.code:
// react-phone-number-input — country dropdown with flags, auto-format, validation
<PhoneInput defaultCountry='US' value={value} onChange={setValue}/>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Full UX (flags, format-as-you-type, paste handling), accessible

v5_principal.code:
// Phone is identity in many markets — primary auth (China, India). Treat as such: country detection from IP, SMS verification flow, opt-in for SMS notifications.
// E.164 storage (+15551234567), localized display.
v5_principal.the_realization: Phone numbers are international identity systems. libphonenumber exists because every country formats differently. E.164 for storage, locale-aware display, country detection. Naive 'type=tel' loses 80% of correctness.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for global apps

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Keyboard users can't reorder, accessibility fail

v2_mid_level.code:
// Up/Down buttons per item
<button onClick={moveUp}>↑</button><button onClick={moveDown}>↓</button>
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Multiple clicks for far moves, no announcement, no focus management

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Keyboard parity with drag, screen reader announces, focus moves with item

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles ARIA, announcements, screen reader UX

v5_principal.code:
// Reordering as multimodal: mouse, keyboard, touch, voice ('move item 3 to position 1'). Same affordance, different inputs. Action-first design.
// Voice commands via Web Speech API.
v5_principal.the_realization: Drag is one input. Keyboard, touch, voice are others. Designing the reorder action and routing inputs to it (vs designing per-input) makes accessibility automatic. Most engineers design drag-first and bolt on keyboard.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 always, v5 for serious products

feature_concept: Toast vs modal vs banner decision

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Friction for cheap actions, interruption

v2_mid_level.code: // Some toasts, some modals — ad hoc
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Inconsistent, designed per-instance

v3_senior.code:
// Codified: cheap+reversible=toast w/ undo; medium=inline confirm; expensive+irreversible=modal w/ type-to-confirm
function notify(action){if(action.reversible&&action.cost<3)return toast(action);if(action.irreversible)return modalTypeConfirm(action);return inlineConfirm(action)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Decision framework, predictable UX, friction matches cost

v4_staff.code:
// Channel hierarchy in design system. Banner (persistent), toast (transient), modal (blocking), inline (contextual). Each has rules.
// Components prevent misuse: <ConfirmAction cost='high' reversible={false}/>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: DS encodes rules, components route to right channel, can't pick wrong

v5_principal.code: // Attention is a budget. Modals spend it; toasts borrow it; banners stake it. Pricing each interruption like CPU time avoids notification fatigue. Track: how many modals/session, dismiss rates.
v5_principal.the_realization: Choosing toast vs modal isn't aesthetic — it's an attention economy. Cheap interruptions for cheap actions, expensive for expensive. Engineers default to modal-for-everything because it's safe; that's how products become annoying.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 in DS, v5 for product polish

scenario: User saves filter+sort+columns config to reuse

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for User saves filter+sort+columns config to reuse: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Users re-set filters every visit

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Single device, no sharing, no defaults

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Cross-device, named, default selectable

v4_staff.code:
// Shared views: 'team views' for org. Permissions (private/team/org). URL-shareable view links.
<Button onClick={()=>copy(`${baseUrl}?view=${view.id}`)}>Share view</Button>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Team collaboration, URL = bookmark, permission model

v5_principal.code:
// Views are queries. Treat as first-class entity. SQL-like (or AST) representation. Version controlled. Linked from dashboards. Sometimes views become reports become alerts.
// Linear/Notion are masters of this.
v5_principal.the_realization: Saved views are the wedge into 'data as workspace'. Once views exist: sharing, defaults, dashboards, alerts. Engineers ship localStorage and miss the platform value. Linear's views are why Linear feels powerful.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for serious tools, v4-v5 for SaaS

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Cmd+K / / opens search: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Users mouse to find search

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Ctrl on Windows? Conflict with browser? '/' alternative?

v3_senior.code:
// Cross-platform + multiple triggers + don't conflict
const isMod=e=>e.metaKey||e.ctrlKey;if(isMod(e)&&e.key==='k')open();else if(e.key==='/'&&!isInputFocused())open()
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Mac+Win, '/' GitHub-style as fallback, not while typing in inputs

v4_staff.code:
// Hotkey library — handles modifiers, sequences, scopes
useHotkeys('mod+k, /',openSearch,{enableOnFormTags:false})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles edge cases, composable shortcuts, scopes

v5_principal.code:
// Shortcuts are product. ?-key shows shortcut help. Customizable. Conflicts with system shortcuts surfaced. Power users live in shortcuts.
// Treat as full feature with discovery (tooltips, help dialog).
v5_principal.the_realization: Shortcuts move users from 'using the product' to 'living in the product'. Engineers add Cmd+K and ship; product teams design the shortcut system: discovery, learning, customization. The latter wins power users (and they evangelize).

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for power-user products

scenario: Upload files directly to S3, bypass app server

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: App server bottleneck, costs egress, max body size limits

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No progress (no XHR), no validation post-upload, no virus scan

v3_senior.code:
// XHR for progress, notify-completion after
const xhr=new XMLHttpRequest();xhr.upload.onprogress=p=>setPct(p.loaded/p.total*100);xhr.open('PUT',signedUrl);xhr.send(file);xhr.onload=async()=>{await api.notifyUploadComplete(uploadId)}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Progress, completion notification triggers server processing (scan, transcode)

v4_staff.code:
// S3 multipart for large files. tus protocol for resume. Lambda triggers on S3 PutObject for processing pipeline.
const {parts}=await initMultipart();// Parallel chunks
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Multipart parallel, resume on failure, event-driven processing

v5_principal.code:
// Storage is a product capability. Uploadthing/UploadCare/Cloudinary handle the system. Frontend = picker; backend = signed URLs; storage = CDN-backed; pipeline = transformations.
// Build vs buy: usually buy for storage.
v5_principal.the_realization: Direct-to-S3 is correct but the pipeline (scan, transcode, deliver, transform) is complex. Most teams should use Uploadthing/Cloudinary and focus on their product. Building the storage system is rarely the right priority.

when_to_use_which_version: v2 minimum, v4-v5 for serious media

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Continues when tab hidden, no pause on hover, no manual control, no keyboard, fast advance dizzy

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No reduced-motion respect, no hover pause, no manual prev/next, no accessibility

v3_senior.code:
// Respect prefers-reduced-motion (pause), pause on hover/focus, dots indicator, prev/next, keyboard
const reduce=useReducedMotion();// Don't autoplay if reduce
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Accessible, motion-aware, hover/focus pause, full controls

v4_staff.code:
// Embla / Swiper — solved carousels. Accessibility, virtual slides, lazy load. const [emblaRef]=useEmblaCarousel({loop:true,...});
...
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Battle-tested, performant, accessible, plugins for autoplay/dots/etc

v5_principal.code:
// Question autoplay. Carousels are widely considered anti-pattern: users miss content, slow load, accessibility headaches. Prefer: explicit pagination, scroll-snap, grid.
// If keeping: make controls obvious, pause aggressive.
v5_principal.the_realization: Auto-advancing carousels are 2010 UX. Modern alternatives (scroll-snap, swipeable cards, grid) communicate better. Accessibility, performance, and engagement studies all favor non-auto. Carousels persist because clients demand them — push back.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum if must, v5 reconsider need

scenario: Select multiple items, drag them together

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Even when multiple selected, drag moves one

v2_mid_level.code:
// Detect: if dragged item is selected, move all selected
onDragStart:(e)=>{if(selectedIds.has(draggedId))e.dataTransfer.setData('ids',Array.from(selectedIds).join(','))}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Drag image shows one item, drop indicator unclear, order semantics unclear

v3_senior.code:
// Custom drag image with count badge, drop reorders all
<DragOverlay>{selected.length>1?<Stack count={selected.length} preview={items[0]}/>:<Card/>}</DragOverlay>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Visual stack, count, drop inserts all at position

v4_staff.code:
// Order in selection matters. Preserve selection order? Original list order? Document the choice.
const moved=selectedInOrder.map(id=>items.find(i=>i.id===id));const remaining=items.filter(i=>!selectedIds.has(i.id));const result=[...remaining.slice(0,dropIdx),...moved,...remaining.slice(dropIdx)]
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Deliberate ordering, edge cases (drop in middle of selection)

v5_principal.code:
// Multi-select drag is a transaction: many items, one operation, undo as unit. Backend takes batch. Optimistic update is one transition.
// Pattern: 'paste' metaphor — cut/copy/paste with keyboard parity.
v5_principal.the_realization: Multi-drag is multi-action UX. Selection model + batch operation + undo + transaction. Engineers treat as 'drag with more items' and miss the consistency of transaction model (especially for undo, error recovery).

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for file managers

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Notify users of new features: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Users don't discover features

v2_mid_level.code:
// Modal on first visit after deploy
{firstVisit&&<Modal>What's new</Modal>}
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Annoying interruption, blocks users who don't care, no targeting

v3_senior.code:
// Subtle indicator (badge on bell), persistent until read, in-app changelog page
<Bell hasNew={unread.length>0}/>;<Sheet><Changelog entries={entries}/></Sheet>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Non-interrupting, discoverable, dismissible, segmented (per feature area)

v4_staff.code:
// Targeted to relevant users (admin sees admin features, devs see API changes). Versioned. Linked to docs. RSS feed for users.
// Tools: Headway, Beamer, custom
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Audience segmentation, integrations, discoverable across surfaces

v5_principal.code:
// Product growth = announce + onboard + measure. Each feature: announce → tutorial → tooltip → in-context tip → analytics on adoption. Linked to roadmap.
// Product-led growth motion.
v5_principal.the_realization: Announcements are a growth surface. Done well: users discover features, adoption climbs, churn drops. Done poorly: interruption fatigue, users blind to changes. The work isn't 'show modal' — it's the full announce-to-adopt funnel.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for SaaS growth
